flashcards.back: A resizable array that automatically grows/shrinks at runtime, managing its own memory on the heap using `malloc`, `realloc`, and `free`.
Capacity is the total allocated space; size is the number of elements used. Capacity is always greater than or equal to size.
Typically doubles capacity when full (e.g., 4 → 8 → 16) to maintain amortized O(1) insertion time.
Uses heap memory for flexibility, unlike stack-based static arrays. This allows for dynamic resizing.
Average time per insertion is constant, even with occasional O(n) resizes. This is achieved through geometric growth (doubling).
When shrinking at half capacity and growing at full capacity, alternating insertions and deletions can cause frequent resizes, leading to O(n) time complexity.
Typically halves capacity when size drops to a quarter of capacity to avoid thrashing.
Efficient insertion and deletion at the end, good cache locality, and ability to handle variable-sized data.
